metadata.title: Why an Agent That Finishes First Can Be Worse Than One That Stops
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of claude-agentic-ai candidate 01. The silent-omission failure mode only — an agent reads partial data and reports complete success, so a confident completion looks identical to a correct one.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL #1F6F5C = read / processed / reached; CRIMSON #BF3339 = unread / skipped / silently missing; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO taxonomy of all eight failure modes (one mechanism only: silent omission); NO prompt injection discussion; NO hallucination probability formalism. Mechanism: agents report success from successful operations only — what they could not reach, could not parse, or skipped is invisible in the completion report.

beats.narration_text: The brief looked good. Six bullets. A clear recommendation. She sent it to leadership.
beats.scene_description: a clean executive brief document on newsprint ground — six crisp bullet points, a confident recommendation at the bottom; slow push-in from bottom-left toward the recommendation line

beats.narration_text: Two days later, a colleague pointed out three dissenting documents in a subfolder the agent never opened. One contradicted the recommendation directly. No error message had appeared anywhere.

production_viz.label: folder tree — three nodes never opened
production_viz.mechanic: a simple folder tree: top node 'project/', five document leaves rendered in TEAL as 'read', three leaves in a subfolder 'dissenting/' rendered in CRIMSON as 'unread'; the summary doc at bottom glows green 'COMPLETE'
production_viz.note: the three CRIMSON nodes are the key — they exist but were never touched

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] folder tree: green summary, three crimson-unread nodes

beats.narration_text: Here is the mechanism. An agent should predict a complete scan. Here is the case where it produced a confident completion report from an incomplete scan. Why?

card.copy: Why does a completion report look identical whether the scan was complete or not?

beats.narration_text: When an agent processes files, it works operation by operation. Read this file: success. Summarize it: success. Move to the next: success. Each step reports its own result.

production_viz.label: operation chain: read -> summarize -> next
production_viz.mechanic: a horizontal chain of three boxes labeled 'READ', 'SUMMARIZE', 'NEXT'; each box fills TEAL and shows a small green checkmark as it completes in sequence; the chain repeats for a second file
production_viz.note: show the per-operation success chain — each step reports only its own result

beats.narration_text: But the agent does not know what it could not reach. A subfolder it lacked access to. A scanned PDF it could not parse. A file it passed over when the folder listing ran long.

production_viz.label: what falls outside the agent's view
production_viz.mechanic: the TEAL operation chain from B04 runs on the left; on the right, three file icons sit in CRIMSON — one labeled 'no access', one 'unreadable scan', one 'not listed' — with a vertical dashed line separating what the agent saw from what it did not
production_viz.note: the key insight: the boundary of what was reached is not the boundary of what existed

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the boundary between reached and unreached

beats.narration_text: A completion report is built from successful operations. It can only list what completed. What was skipped leaves no entry.

card.copy: The completion report sees only what completed.

beats.narration_text: So the report says: twenty-three files processed, brief generated, task complete. And that is entirely accurate — for the twenty-three files the agent reached. The three it never opened are simply absent from the account.

production_viz.label: 23 reported, 26 in scope
production_viz.mechanic: two rows: top row is TEAL squares labeled '23 processed'; bottom row shows '23 TEAL + 3 CRIMSON' squares labeled '26 in folder'; the 3 CRIMSON squares on the bottom have no corresponding entry in the top row — they simply do not appear
production_viz.note: the 3 crimson squares have no entry in the report row above — the silence is the failure

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] 23 in report vs 26 in folder — 3 never appear

beats.narration_text: Maya ran an agent over twelve client PDFs to draft a digest. The agent read nine. Three were scanned images it could not parse. It produced a confident brief and reported done. The three skipped PDFs held the Q4 target revision. The digest shipped with the old targets.

production_viz.label: Maya: 12 PDFs, 9 read, 3 skipped
production_viz.mechanic: twelve file icons in a grid: nine fill TEAL with a small check, three remain CRIMSON labeled 'scan — unreadable'; an arrow from the nine leads to a 'DIGEST' document with 'old targets'; a label 'Q4 revision' points to one of the three CRIMSON files
production_viz.note: illustrative example — numbers labeled illustrative in FACTCHECK

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] Maya: 9/12 read, digest ships wrong targets

beats.narration_text: The practice: before you accept a completion report, ask for an inventory. How many items were in scope? How many were actually processed? How many failed or were skipped? If those numbers do not match, the report is incomplete.

production_viz.label: the three inventory questions
production_viz.mechanic: three stacked rows, each a question in SERIF with a number field: 'In scope: ?' -> fills with a number; 'Processed: ?' -> fills with a number; 'Skipped/failed: ?' -> fills with a number; when skipped is nonzero, that row highlights CRIMSON
production_viz.note: animate each question appearing in sequence; the skipped row lights CRIMSON to show the gap

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] three inventory questions animate in sequence

beats.narration_text: A confident completion is not evidence of a complete scan. An inventory check is. The count mismatch is the signal.

card.copy: The count mismatch is the signal.

beats.narration_text: A crash tells you something went wrong. A confident completion might not. The agent that stops and flags a parsing failure is giving you information. The one that silently skips it and reports done is not.

production_viz.label: crash vs silent omission
production_viz.mechanic: two columns side by side: left 'CRASH' column shows a red error box — the problem is visible; right 'SILENT OMISSION' column shows a green 'COMPLETE' box — the problem is invisible; arrow under right column points to 'gap in brief' in CRIMSON
production_viz.note: the whole point of the title — a crash announces itself; silent omission does not

beats.narration_text: A completion report should predict a complete scan. Here is why it does not: the report is built from successful operations, not from a census of everything that existed. Ask for the inventory, not just the result.

card.copy: Ask for the inventory, not just the result.
